nameMeanings.name: Rodriguez
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Manuel, Luis, Carlos, Jesus, Francisco, Jorge, Pedro, Raul, Miguel, Roberto, Ramon, Rafael.  Portuguese Wenceslao, Ligia, Catarina, Paulo, Joaquim, Omero, Joao, Wenseslao, Adauto, Albeiro, Godofredo.

Spanish (Rodríguez) and Portuguese: patronymic from the personal name Rodrigo . Rodriguez (including in the sense 2 below) is the ninth most frequent surname in the US. Compare De Rodriguez and Rodriquez .

Jewish (Sephardic): adoption of the Iberian surname (see above) at the moment of conversion to Roman Catholicism. After the return to Judaism (generations later), some descendants retained the name their families used as Catholics.

variants.name: Rodiguez
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Luis, Jorge, Jose, Juan, Pedro, Raul, Adriana, Alvaro, Ana Luz, Angel, Armando, Arnulfo.
Hispanic: altered form of Spanish Rodríguez (see Rodriguez ).

variants.name: Rodriquez
meanings: Spanish (Rodríquez): variant of Rodríguez (see Rodriguez ). This surname is very rare in Spain.

variants.name: Rodriges
meanings: Hispanic: altered form of Spanish Rodríguez (see Rodriguez ).

variants.name: Rodreguez
meanings: Hispanic: altered form of Spanish Rodríguez (see Rodriguez ).

variants.name: Rodrigo
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Manuel, Elena, Rafael, Alfonso, Alicia, Angel, Araceli, Benardo, Carlos, Carlota, Cristina, Emilio.  Indian Devi, Dileep, Dilip, Gamini, Mahendra, Manoj, Sunil, Tilak.

Spanish and Portuguese: from the personal name Rodrigo, from ancient Germanic Hrōdrīc, a compound of hrōd ‘fame, renown’ + rīc ‘power, kingdom’. Rodrigo is one of the most important Spanish personal names of ancient Germanic origin. It was borne by the last of the Visigoth kings and by a Christian saint, Saint Roderick, one of the 9th-century martyrs of Córdoba. This surname is also found in and around some of the former Portuguese territories of western India.

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Osorio
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Carlos, Juan, Luis, Francisco, Jorge, Miguel, Mario, Jaime, Manuel, Rafael, Ana.  Portuguese Agostinho, Marcio.

Spanish; Portuguese (Osório): from a medieval personal name Osorius, of uncertain origin. It is perhaps a metathesized form of Latin Orosius (Greek Orosios, a derivative of oros ‘mountain’), the name borne by a 4th-century Iberian theologian and historian, who was famous in Spain throughout the Middle Ages. Compare Ozorio .

variants.name: Osornio
meanings: Hispanic (Mexico): probably an altered form of Spanish   Osorio  , influenced by   Osorno  .

variants.name: Osoria
meanings: Spanish: from the personal name Osoria, feminine form of Osorio . This surname is most common in the Dominican Republic, Mexico, and Cuba. Compare Ozoria .

variants.name: Ozorio
meanings: Spanish; Portuguese (Ozório): variant of Osorio .

nameMeanings.name: Villalobos
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Juan, Jesus, Luis, Carlos, Francisco, Manuel, Ruben, Javier, Rafael, Mario, Ramon.
Spanish: habitational name from Villalobos in Zamora province, named from villa ‘(outlying) farmstead, (dependent) settlement’ (see Villa ) + lobos, plural of lobo ‘wolf’.

variants.name: Villalovos
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jesus, Maurillo, Adolfo, Alfonso, Apolonia, Arcadio, Armando, Bernardo, Celso, Dionicio, Domingo, Ezequiel.
Spanish: variant of   Villalobos  .

variants.name: Villa
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Juan, Manuel, Francisco, Jesus, Carlos, Luis, Mario, Miguel, Raul, Pedro, Ramon.  Italian Antonio, Lorenzo, Angelo, Dario, Federico, Luciano, Dante, Leonardo, Marco, Mauro, Salvatore, Vito.

Spanish: topographic name from villa ‘(outlying) farmstead, (dependent) settlement’ (see 2 below), or a habitational name from any of the places (mainly in Asturias) called Villa, or from any of numerous places called with this as the first element. Compare Billa and Lavilla .

Italian: topographic name for someone who lived in a village as opposed to an isolated farmhouse, or in a town as opposed to the countryside, from Latin villa ‘country house, estate’, a term later used to denote a group of houses forming a settlement and in some dialects to denote the most important area or center of a settlement. This may also be a habitational name from any of various places called with villa. Compare Davilla .

nameMeanings.name: Rodrigo
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Visigothic: composed of the elements hrōd ‘fame’ + rīc ‘power’. It was the name of the last king of the Visigoths, defeated by the Moors in 711 .

en.lifeSummary: When Conde Rodrigo Rodriguez de Osorio Y Villalobos was born in 1200, his father, Martin Osorio Alfonso de Villalobos Gonzalez, was 34 and his mother, Mrs Martin Osorio Alfonso de Villalobos Gonzalez, was 25. He had at least 1 son with Mayor Alvarez De Asturias. He died before 1261.
